coredata.dc:title: Indirect colorimetric detection of glutathione based on its radical restoration ability using carbon nanodots as nanozymes
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shamsipur, M.
Safavi, A.
Mohammadpour, Z.
coredata.dc:description: This work addresses a colorimetric sensor for glutathione (GSH). In this study carbon nanodots (CDs) were used as the peroxidase mimetic catalysts. The blue colored free cation radicals were generated simply by an oxidation reaction of tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) which was catalyzed by CDs as the catalysts. So, the key role of the CDs was the enhancement in radical generation thereby enhancing the system sensitivity. The results in this study showed that the free radical cation concentration was inversely related to the GSH concentration in the range of 0–7μM with a detection limit of 0.3μM. Moreover, this system showed a perfect selectivity over non-thiolated biological molecules. As a practical application, this chemosensor was used to measure the GSH content of the whole human blood with the results consistent with that of a classical Ellman method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
Carbon nanodots
Glutathione
Chemosensor
Hydrogen peroxide
Tetramethylbenzidine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of substrate working solution and kinetic measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative TEM micrograph (A) and absorption and photoluminescence spectra (B) of the aqueous solution of CDs.

Absorption curves of the reaction products of TMB oxidation in the absence (a) and presence (b) of CDs 1000s after the reaction started. Inset: The time dependant change in absorption spectra of cation radical signals at 370 (—▴) and 653nm (—●).
Dependency of the C-Dots catalytic activity on (A) pH, (B) temperature, (C) H2O2 concentration and D) C-Dots concentration. The maximum point in each curve was set as 100%.

Absorption spectra of TMB reaction mixture under different conditions in sodium acetate buffer (pH 3.5): (a) Stopped reaction mixture by sodium fluoride (50μL of 0.2%). (b) After addition of a mixture of four amino acids (His, Ser, Arg, Glu) each of them 20μM to (a). (c) Spectrum taken immediately after addition of GSH (20μM) to (b). (d) Spectrum taken 1min later after (c). Inset: The gradual decrease of the absorbance at 653nm after stopping the catalytic oxidation reaction with sodium fluoride.

(A) Time course monitoring of the reaction at 653nm (Inset: The absorbance versus concentration curve in a fixed time mode, 900s after the reaction started. To obtain the real peak intensity, the absorbance value at 800nm (the point where the spectrophotometer starts to record the spectrum) was subtracted from the absorbance value at 653nm). (B) Effect of GSH on the absorption spectra with different GSH concentrations ranging from 0 to 15μM in the fixed time mode, 900s after start of the reaction.

Colorimetric response of the system toward biothiols and other biomolecules (2μM) including glucose (Glc), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), methionine (Met), tryptophan (Trp), phenylalanine (Phe), arginine (Arg), histidine (His), serine (Ser), S-(2-aminoethyl)-l-cysteine (AEC), bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-acetyl-l-cysteine (NAC), cysteaminium chloride (CYA-HCl). The reaction was performed in acetate buffer (pH 3.5, 0.1M), CDs (80μgmL−1), TMB (50μM) and H2O2 (1mM).
(A) Three different forms of TMB in acidic pH and (B) mechanism of TMB oxidation by H2O2 and repair of the produced cation radical with GSH.
Results of recovery studies of the analysis of GSH in human blood.

head.ce:title: Indirect colorimetric detection of glutathione based on its radical restoration ability using carbon nanodots as nanozymes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shiraz University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Razi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work addresses a colorimetric sensor for glutathione (GSH). In this study carbon nanodots (CDs) were used as the peroxidase mimetic catalysts. The blue colored free cation radicals were generated simply by an oxidation reaction of tetramethyl benzidine (TMB) which was catalyzed by CDs as the catalysts. So, the key role of the CDs was the enhancement in radical generation thereby enhancing the system sensitivity. The results in this study showed that the free radical cation concentration was inversely related to the GSH concentration in the range of 0–7μM with a detection limit of 0.3μM. Moreover, this system showed a perfect selectivity over non-thiolated biological molecules. As a practical application, this chemosensor was used to measure the GSH content of the whole human blood with the results consistent with that of a classical Ellman method.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH, l-γ-glutamyl-l-cysteinyl-glycine) is an important intracellular tripeptide with the widespread utility in plants, mammals, fungi and some prokaryotic organisms [1]. As the most abundant intracellular small molecule thiol, GSH plays a key role in the detoxification of a variety of electrophilic compounds and peroxides because it is a substrate or cofactor for a number of protective enzymes, such as GSH peroxidase [2]. An imbalance of GSH levels is associated with a wide range of pathologies including cancer, aging, or heart problems [3,4]. Thus, it is important to be able to monitor the change of GSH concentration in biological fluids. Among the techniques for GSH analysis including high performance liquid chromatography [5–7] and capillary electrophoresis [8] which involve cumbersome laboratory procedures and require expensive and sophisticated instrumentation, optical methods seem to be the most convenient ones for thiols detection [9–13]. Specific reactions between the optical probes and thiols are associated with the marked increase/decrease in the fluorescence or colorimetric signals [14–17]. Enzymatic recycling methods [18] are the most common colorimetric probes for measurement of total GSH. Despite the fact that these methods are very effective, natural enzymes in these works need to be separated and purified carefully.

With the massive development in nanotechnology, opportunities and possibilities for designing the new generations of sensing tools have been provided. The targeted sensing of biomolecules has become a major research thrust in the last decade [19–22]. Assays based on gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) assembly have received considerable attention for the determination of thiolated biomolecules on account of the fact that AuNPs aggregate upon the addition of these compounds [23,24].

Carbon nanodots (CDs) which constitute a fascinating class of semiconductor nanocrystals, have drawn much attention in recent years [25]. Their attractive features namely, chemical inertness, lack of blinking, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility make them superior compared with organic dyes and traditional semiconductor based quantum dots in a host of applications [26–28].

Enzyme mimetic function is one of the interesting features of nanomaterials which was first exhibited by magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) [29]. Since then, more and more nanoparticles have been evaluated as enzyme mimics [30,31]. Although MNPs are promising candidates for use as nanozymes, there are few problems associated with their application. Some of these materials have complicated fabrication processes. For example, the fabrication of BiFeO3 nanoparticles needs several steps [32]; a glove box with N2 saturated atmosphere is necessary in the formation of nanostructured FeS [33]. Therefore, easy-to-prepare material is necessary for designing catalytic assays. Moreover, since MNPs are heterogeneous catalysts, they should be well dispersed in aqueous solution and properly removed by a magnet [34], both are time consuming operations. As another Fe-based peroxidase mimics, G-quadruplex-hemin complexes are the multicomponent catalysts with multistep preparation [13]. The performance of these complexes is entirely dependent on the presence of potassium ions. Other heterogeneous enzyme mimics like single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) [35] and helical carbon nanotubes (HCNTs) [36] should be functionalized on the surface to enhance their water solubility. Furthermore, the purification of CNTs is a laborious process. CDs as the carbon based nanozymes, exhibit excellent water solubility and act as the homogenous catalysts. Efficient methods capable of large scale production of CDs from the cheap carbon sources have been reported in the literature [37]. Moreover, they are biocompatible, so can be regarded as the disposable catalysts. Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of CDs was first discovered by Shi et al. [38]. In the presence of H2O2, CDs could catalyze the oxidation of the peroxidase substrates and produce a typical color reaction. An increased sensitivity in color development caused by catalytic property of CDs allows the colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose in biological samples.

In this work, we utilized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a widely used redox indicator in enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay systems [39]. In the presence of CDs, as peroxidase mimetic catalysts, this substrate produces a blue colored free radical cation upon oxidation by H2O2. On the basis of hydrogen donating ability of GSH in restoration of damaged molecules [2], it could successfully repair the free radical cation and reconstruct TMB. This system encouraged us to design a new colorimetric sensor for GSH detection based on a kinetic fixed time method.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments. Acetate buffer solution (0.1M, pH 3.5) was prepared from sodium acetate (Merck) and acetic acid (Riedel-deHaën). The buffer solution was filtered through the syringe filters with the pore size of 0.2μm (Minisart). Stock solution of TMB (Merck) was prepared in DMSO and stored in the refrigerator at a tightly closed sample tube. The stock solution of H2O2 was prepared daily by appropriate dilution of H2O2 30% (Merck) to the final concentration of 10mM. GSH stock solution (1mM) was prepared freshly in deionized water and kept at 4°C during the experiments. Ethylene diamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (Na2EDTA·2H2O) was used as the precursor for synthesis of CDs.

A tube furnace (Azar Furnace-Iran equipped with gas flow lines) was utilized for the salt pyrolysis. The absorption spectra were recorded by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) using 1cm path length cuvette. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Cary eclipse 8510175900 Varian spectrophotometer. Temperature in all experiments was kept constant at 30°C within ±1°C using LO-Temptrol 154 thermostat. Size and shape of CDs were characterized by a Zeiss-EM10C transmission electron microscope (TEM) operating at 80kV.

CDs were prepared according to the reported procedure [40]. Briefly, a crucible filled with 0.5g Na2EDTA·2H2O was heated in a tube furnace using an argon flow at 400°C for 2h. The resulting black powder was dissolved in acetone (20mL) and then centrifuged at high speed (14,000rpm) for 15min. Evaporation of the upper yellow solution resulted in the CDs powder. Finally, the powder was redissolved in 1mL of deionized water for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of substrate working solution and kinetic measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Substrate working solution was freshly prepared 5–10min before use. To the final volume of 2.5mL of acetate buffer (0.1M pH 3.5) in the cuvette, a mixture containing TMB (50μM), CD (80μgmL−1) and GSH (different amounts) was added. The mixture was incubated in the cuvette for 5min in order to reach the optimum temperature (30°C). The reaction was started by the addition of H2O2 to make a final concentration of 1mM. The reaction product was monitored at 653nm.

For preparation of the real sample, human venous blood was collected and hemolyzed in 0.01M sodium phosphate/0.005M EDTA, pH 7.5. The hemolyzate was frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −70°C before analysis.

TEM image of the as-synthesized CDs was verified as near spherical nanoparticles. The size of the nanoparticles was relatively small, with the majority of particles falling below 10nm in diameter (Fig. 1A). In addition to TEM image, photoluminescence spectrum of CDs confirmed their synthesis by the reported procedure. Fig. 1B shows the typical absorption and photoluminescence spectra of CDs in aqueous solution.

As it is seen, CDs show obvious optical absorption in the UV region, with a tail extending to the visible range. When excited in 330nm, CDs exhibited strong photoluminescence centered at 400nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of about 80nm. The maximum photoluminescence intensity was observed at the excitation wavelength of 330nm which was in agreement with the presence of an absorption shoulder at about 310nm. By further increase in the excitation wavelength, the emission of the CDs shifted from 400 to 490nm which was accompanied by a remarkable photoluminescence intensity decrease. The dependence of emission wavelength and intensity upon the excitation wavelength is a unifying feature of CDs [26].

TMB is a widely used chromogen for horseradish peroxidase (HRP) based detection systems, because it yields reaction products with high absorption coefficients and low carcinogenicity [41]. In the presence of HRP or peroxidase like catalysts, H2O2 oxidizes TMB in two electron transfer steps. In the first step, a one-electron oxidation yields the TMB free radical cation. The presence of this radical cation has been proved by electron spin resonance spectroscopy in the previous works [41,42]. Two of the intermediate radical cations form a charge-transfer complex which is responsible for the blue color (λ max of 653 and 370nm) observed during the oxidation of TMB. In the second electron transfer step, the cation radical is further oxidized to form the yellow colored diimine derivative of TMB (λ max 450nm). Thus, the absorption spectrum of the oxidation product of TMB shows three peaks at 370nm, 450nm and 653nm. An equilibrium state that depends on the reaction conditions exists between the charge transfer complex and diimine derivative.

Several distinct mechanisms of protection against radicals by GSH are identified [2]. These include radical scavenging [43], restoration of damaged molecules by hydrogen donation [44], reduction of peroxides [45] and maintenance of protein thiols in the reduced state [46]. Hydrogen donation to radicals for restoring the ionized molecules to their original form appears to be the most important mechanism [47]. Herein, this type of protection works for the sensing application. The blue colored TMB radical cations were effectively quenched by GSH. These radical cations were repaired into the original TMB by means of the restoration ability of GSH.

To demonstrate the peroxidase-like activity of the as prepared CDs, oxidation of TMB with H2O2 in the presence and absence of CDs as the catalyst was tested. Fig. 2 shows that similar to HRP, CDs could catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce a blue colored reaction product. As previously reported, the nature of peroxidase-like activity of the CDs may originate from their catalytic ability in H2O2 reduction. Increasing charge density of CDs as a result of electron transfer from the lone pair electrons of the TMB amino group to CDs caused acceleration of electron transfer from CDs to H2O2, which in turn resulted in enhanced reaction rate of TMB oxidation by H2O2 [38]. As mentioned above, the free radical cation generated during the TMB oxidation shows two absorption bands at 370 and 653nm. Although the change in analytical signal (see the inset of Fig. 2) is larger at 370nm, this wavelength is not uniquely assigned to the radical cation. In fact this band overlaps with the two adjacent ones (i.e. the band maximum at 285nm which is ascribed to TMB and 450nm due to diimine derivative of TMB). Since the basic principle in this work is based on the radical cation concentration, as designated by its signal intensity, we selected 653nm as the wavelength which exclusively attributes to the free ionized radical. The catalytic activity of CDs is dependent on pH, temperature and H2O2 concentration. Under the optimal conditions, it was revealed that the maximum catalytic activity of CDs occurred in a sodium acetate buffer of pH 3.5 at 30°C and 1mM H2O2 (Fig. 3 ). All the experiments were performed with a single batch of C-Dots. C-Dots maintained the catalytic activity well enough for at least three months and signal reproducibility was good.

The ability of GSH on the restoration of free radicals generated during the TMB oxidation was examined in a preliminary test (Fig. 4 ). For this purpose, a mixture containing H2O2, TMB and CDs was incubated for about one hour (in fact TMB free cation radical was prepared in batch). The blue color emerged with the maximum absorbance at 653nm. As 50% turnover of the initial amount of TMB reached, the reaction was terminated by the addition of an appropriate amount of sodium fluoride. Addition of a mixture containing four amino acids (His, Ser, Arg, Glu) immediately after stopping the color development did not affect the maximum absorbance at 653nm, whereas GSH could effectively quench the radical cation after a very short time period. In fact, the observed simultaneous decrease in the cation radical peak intensity and increased peak intensity of TMB is in support of the cation radical repair by GSH. In this experiment, a batch of blue colored cation radical was prepared. After a certain time period, the color development was stopped by the addition of sodium fluoride as the inhibitor. It was expected that addition of sodium fluoride would terminate the reaction. Therefore, the absorption band at 653nm was expected to be constant over time. But, after stopping the reaction, the absorption band at 653nm decreased gradually. This experiment was completed within 4min, which corresponds to about 1.4% decrease of the initial signal at 653nm (please see the inset of Fig. 4). The inset of Fig. 4 shows that after 45min (the minimum total duration time needed for performing the whole analysis) about 9% of the initial signal was decreased. This could cause a systematic error in the analysis. Therefore; we changed the measurement strategy and performed a competitive oxidation reaction, in which GSH was added to the solution prior to H2O2 addition.

In commercial ELISA kits with peroxidase as the natural enzyme and TMB as the chromogenic substrate, fluoride has been known to act as the blue stop solution. Although, natural enzyme inhibitors are widely investigated in the literature [48–50], the inhibitory effect on nanostructured enzyme mimics have been rarely studied. There are only a few reports on activators and inhibitors to tailor the catalytic activity of nanozymes [51,52]. Therefore much study is needed to elucidate the molecular mechanism involved in artificial enzyme inhibition mechanisms. As it was mentioned above, the C-Dots in the present work were synthesized according to the previously reported procedure [40]. The surface of these nanostructures are covered with carboxylate groups. In the previous reports on peroxidase mimetic properties of carboneous nanomaterials, it has been suggested that the presence of carboxyl groups on the surface of carbon nanostructures accounts for the catalytic reduction of H2O2 [38]. In the present system it was observed that after addition of fluoride, the absorbance at 652nm was decreased over time. We believe that fluoride interacts with carboxyl groups on the surface of C-Dots and block them. This prevents their catalytic activity.

A mechanism is suggested which clears the role of GSH in this system. From the concentration-pH diagram of TMB [53] it has been revealed that in pH 3.5 of sodium acetate buffer, this molecule exists in three forms (I (4%), II (46%) and III (50%)) (Scheme 1A). For oxidizing this molecule to the corresponding radical cation, it is necessary for the amino group to be deprotonated. So, form III cannot participate in the oxidation process. One electron oxidation of forms I and II, results in removal of one electron from the free amino groups of these forms. According to Scheme 1B, restoration of the two kinds of TMB cation radicals to their original molecules is accompanied by converting GSH to GS. Restoration ability of GSH by hydrogen donation has been confirmed by many researchers [54]. Hydrogen donating to the cation radicals which are derived from I and II, turn them to original TMB molecules in the form of II and III. The mechanism of sensing can have two possible routes. One mechanism is the one suggested in the present work which is based on the hydrogen donation to blue colored radical cations and restoring them to their original colorless state. Another mechanism is suggested in a report on the oxidation of cysteine by H2O2, which prevents the catalysis of the 2,20-azino-bis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonicacid (ABTS)–H2O2 reaction by G-quadruplex halves [13]. It seems that both mechanisms can prevail because the results of both are the same.
In a competitive experiment, the effect of GSH on the reaction progress was evaluated by the addition of GSH to a solution containing TMB and CDs, before the start of the reaction.

When the reaction triggered by H2O2, a decrease in the reaction rate was observed in a certain time interval. Comparison of reaction profiles in the presence and absence of initial GSH, clearly shows that GSH could successfully impede the radical generation while no significant change occurred in the absence of GSH (Fig. 5A). The result of this experiment which is fully described in the next paragraph confirmed that the TMB radical cation restoration is attributed to GSH. This property provided us the opportunity to design a colorimetric sensor for GSH detection based on a kinetic fixed time method.

In a series of competitive measurements, different concentrations of GSH with fixed amounts of TMB and CDs in sodium acetate buffer solution of pH 3.5 were incubated in a cuvette thermostated at 30°C for 5min. The kinetic profiles of the reaction, monitored at 653nm, at different GSH concentrations ranging from 0 to 15μM are shown in Fig. 5A from which a time period of 900s was selected as the optimum reaction time, based on both the sensitivity and linearity of the resulting absorbance-time plots. As indicated in Fig. 5B, in the fixed time mode, the absorbance band intensity centered on 653nm is inversely proportional to the GSH concentration. The linear calibration plot is presented in the inset of Fig. 5A. This shows that increasing the GSH concentration enhanced its radical restoration activity. Under the optimized experimental conditions, a linear relationship was obtained over the concentration range of 0–7μM with a detection limit of 0.3μM comparable [12] or even better [55] than the previous reports.

To investigate the selectivity of the designed chemosensor, in a series of competitive experiments, the change in absorbance of a 2μM GSH was evaluated under optimized experimental conditions in the presence of equimolar concentrations of other possible interfering thiols and biomolecules specifically amino acids. The results are shown in Fig. 6 . As it is obvious, a significant feature of the present system is its ability to detect GSH in the presence of various other amino acids.

The changes induced by non-thiolated compounds were rather low. The two sulfur containing molecules i.e., methionine and S-(2-aminoethyl)-l-cysteine in which sulfur atom is attached to two adjacent methyl groups do not show any interference while for N-acetyl-l-cysteine a dramatic decrease in the signal intensity was observed indicating that the thiol functionality is essential for spectral changes.

According to what has been reported in the literature [2], the reaction rates of GSH, cysteine and cysteamine with various radicals are almost comparable to each other. The excellent selectivity of the system for GSH over the tested non-thiolated molecules could be a result of the great hydrogen donation power of sufhydryl group in repairing the ionized TMB. However, as the GSH concentration in the human whole blood reaches to millimolar ranges [56], coexistence of GSH with other thiolated molecules is simply circumvented by appropriate dilution of the real sample which effectively eliminates the possible interference of other thiolated compounds.
A standard addition method was adopted to assess the method reliability, and the samples were directly spiked with certain amounts of GSH standard solution.

For assay, 10μL of hemolyzate was added to a cuvette containing a mixture of CDs/TMB in sodium acetate buffer, pH 3.5. Known concentrations of GSH were added to the cuvette. The reaction was started by the addition of H2O2 and the rate of color development at 653nm was followed. The concentration of GSH in the whole blood sample with added standards was determined, and analytical recoveries were calculated which are shown in Table 1 . As seen, this result is in good agreement with that of the modified Ellman method [57]. A key advantage of the present method compared with Ellman reagent in the analysis of the whole blood is elimination of hemoglobin interference because the absorption band of Ellman reagent coincides with the hemoglobin soret band at 410nm. This makes the sensitivity of the method to be decreased [57] whereas the measured wavelength in this method is 653nm far from the hemoglobin soret band. The selectivity of the present system depends to some extent on the type of real sample selected. The concentration of GSH in human blood (millimolar levels) is much higher than either cysteine or Hcy concentration (micro molar levels). The linear range in the present work is in the micromolar range. In order to bring the concentration of GSH suitable for analysis with this system, about 1000 times dilution was done. Therefore, the concentration of cysteine and Hcy become negligible, far beyond the LOD value.

In summary, a colorimetric sensor based on the hydrogen donating ability of GSH in restoration of radicals was developed. CDs with their peroxidase mimetic activity catalyze the oxidation reaction and hence enhance the sensitivity of the method by acceleration of color development. Interference studies revealed that the sensor presented high selectivity over non-thiolated compounds. Corresponding calibration plot of absorbance versus GSH concentration was linear in the range from 0 to 7μM with the correlation coefficient of 0.998. The theoretical detection limit of the method was determined as 0.3μM. Analysis of real assays suggested that this sensor was of perfect performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude to Iran's National Elites Foundation and Shiraz University Research Council for the support of this work.

Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Carbon dots as nontoxic and high-performance fluorescence imaging agents
Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles as peroxidase mimetics and their applications in H2O2 and glucose detection

Research progress of nanoparticles as enzyme mimetics

Nanostructured FeS as a mimic peroxidase for biocatalysis and biosensing
Intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of ferromagnetic nanoparticles
Label-free colorimetric detection of single nucleotide polymorphism by using single-walled carbon nanotube intrinsic peroxidase-like activity
Helical carbon nanotubes: intrinsic peroxidase catalytic activity and its application for biocatalysis and biosensing
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Carbon nanodots as peroxidase mimetics and their applications to glucose detection
A stable and highly sensitive 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine-based substrate reagent for enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Some aspects of the chromogen 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine as hydrogen donor in a horseradish peroxidase assay
Protection of transforming DNA from X-irradiation-induced damage by OH scavengers
Int. J. Radiat. Biol. Relat. Stud. Phys. Chem. Med.
Lipid antioxidants: how they may act in biological systems
The role of glutathione transferase in the detoxification and repair of lipid and DNA hydroperoxides
Radiosensitization by diamide analogs and arsenicals
Int. J. Radiat. Oncol. Biol. Phys.
Free radical repair mechanisms and the interactions of glutathione and vitamins C and E
Effect of sodium fluoride on certain enzymes and proline content in Cenchrus leaves
Effect of fluoride on catalase, guiacol peroxidase and ascorbate oxidase activities in two verities of mulberry leaves (Morus alba L.)
The influence of different fluoride salts on fluoride-mediated inhibition of peroxidase activity in human saliva

Tuning, inhibiting and restoring the enzyme mimetic activities of Pt-apoferritin

Blood and plasma glutathione measured in healthy subjects by HPLC: relation to sex, aging, biological variables, and life habits
Improved determination of acetylcholinesterase activity in human whole blood

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mojtaba Shamsipur has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at Michigan State University in 1979. He is currently a professor of chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran. His research work has been mainly focused on the thermodynamic and kinetic studies of macrocyclic ligand complexes, and their analytical applications in areas such as ion-transport through liquid membranes, solid-phase extraction, ion-selective-electrodes and optical sensors. He has published over 500 scientific papers in international journals.

Afsaneh Safavi received her Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry in 1980 from Birmingham University, Birmingham, UK. She is currently Professor of Analytical Chemistry with Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. Her current research interests include electrochemical and optical sensors and nanosensors, nanochemistry and environmental chemistry.

Zahra Mohammadpour received a B.S. in Pure Chemistry from Yasuj University in 2008 and an M.S. in Analytical Chemistry from Shiraz University in 2010. Currently she is a Ph.D. student at Shiraz University. Her interests include investigation of semiconductor quantum dots in chemical sensing technology.
